summary: I lived in Fayette County later in my life, especially when I worked as the environmental commissioner for the city of Lexington, which is in Fayette County. It was a challenging time because we went under an EPA consent decree to manage storm water properly after some tragic drownings near the football stadium. I also helped initiate recycling programs and bought new garbage trucks to improve waste management there. Fayette County was where I could see the direct impact of my environmental work at a local level.

I was actually born in Laurel County because there was no medical facility in Clay County at the time. Although I didn't grow up there, my birth in Laurel County marks the beginning of my story in Eastern Kentucky. It was a rural area, much like the coal communities my family was connected to.

I grew up in Clay County, specifically in Manchester, Kentucky, where my dad worked for the local coal company. Clay County was a coal mining community, and many of my male ancestors were coal miners or farmers there. It was a tough place to live as the coal industry began to decline in the 1960s, which eventually pushed my family to move to Louisville for better opportunities.

Manchester, Kentucky, was where I spent my childhood in Clay County. It was a small town deeply connected to the coal mining industry, where my father worked as an accountant for the mine. Growing up there shaped my early experiences before we moved to Louisville due to the decline in coal jobs.

My dad was a World War II veteran, and after the war, he took advantage of the GI Bill to go to college. His service shaped his life and career, eventually leading him to become an accountant for the coal mine. The war's aftermath influenced my family's opportunities and mobility in Eastern Kentucky.

My dad was born in Pineville, Kentucky, and later lived in Harlan. Both places are coal communities in Eastern Kentucky, which is where my family roots are. These towns represent the coal mining culture that influenced my upbringing and family history.

Harlan, Kentucky, is another coal town where my dad lived after being born in Pineville. It was part of the coal mining region that shaped my family's life and work. These communities were tightly knit but faced economic challenges as the coal industry declined.

When the coal industry declined in the 1960s, my family moved from Manchester to Louisville, which was a huge culture shock for me. I struggled academically in the Jefferson County school system because I wasn't prepared for the jump. Louisville was where my dad found new work, and we stayed there until I was in high school before moving again to Knoxville.

Jefferson County is where Louisville is located, and I attended school there after moving from Eastern Kentucky. The school system was a big adjustment for me because I was behind academically compared to other students. It was a formative period before my family moved to Knoxville, Tennessee.

After Louisville, my family moved to Knoxville, Tennessee, where I graduated from high school. I started college early at the University of Tennessee but soon transferred back to the University of Kentucky because I preferred the blue over the orange. Knoxville was an important transitional place in my education and life.

I began my college education at the University of Tennessee, starting a year early, but I quickly transferred back to the University of Kentucky because I liked the school better. The University of Tennessee was my first taste of college life after high school in Knoxville. It was a stepping stone in my academic journey.

The University of Kentucky is where I completed my degrees, starting with social work and later mechanical engineering. I really liked the school and the blue color better than the University of Tennessee's orange. UK was also where I managed hospital maintenance planning later in my career and worked on environmental projects.

I worked at Procter & Gamble for almost twenty-four years, starting right out of college. My early work was in engineering, operations, and maintenance, but later I took on environmental permitting and safety programs. P&G was progressive in promoting women, and I gained many certifications there. I also helped start recycling systems and celebrated Earth Day at the company.

At Procter & Gamble, we celebrated Earth Day for the first time, which was a new initiative back then. It was part of our efforts to improve environmental awareness and start recycling programs. Earth Day marked a shift in how the company approached environmental responsibility during my career.

Kentucky has been central to my life and career, from growing up in its coal communities to working as the state's first female environmental commissioner. I worked for the Kentucky state government under Commissioner Lloyd Cress and later led the environmental division. Representing Kentucky on a national level with the EPA was a proud part of my work.

Kentucky EXCEL was a voluntary leadership program I helped start while working in the Kentucky state environmental office. It was designed for people who wanted to promote environmentally conscientious work and behavior. The program was part of our efforts to educate and engage the public and businesses in better environmental practices.

As Kentucky's environmental commissioner, I regularly met with EPA Region 4 leaders and represented the state on national environmental issues. The EPA sets federal laws that Kentucky implements through authorized programs in air, waste, and water. I also worked to bring Lexington into compliance with EPA storm water regulations after a federal consent decree.

Ernie Fletcher was the Republican governor I worked for as Kentucky's environmental commissioner. Despite political differences—I'm a liberal Democrat—he supported many environmental initiatives and valued my work. It was a positive experience working under his leadership.

Jim Newberry hired me to be the first environmental commissioner for the city of Lexington after Governor Fletcher lost the election. Working for Jim was one of the best jobs I've ever had because I could see the direct impact of my work locally. He was a magnificent person to work for and supported environmental progress in the city.

Lexington, Kentucky, is where I served as the city's first environmental commissioner. I took the job when the city was under an EPA consent decree to fix storm water issues after two young women drowned near the football stadium. I led efforts to improve storm water management, sewer systems, recycling, and air quality programs. It was rewarding to see tangible environmental improvements in Lexington.

Although I mentioned working for a private engineering company based in Cincinnati, I did not specify Rockwell International in my story. Therefore, I have no direct connection or experience to share about Rockwell International from my career or life.

Paducah, Kentucky, was mentioned as one of the locations of state environmental offices, but I did not work there directly. It is one of the field office locations for the Kentucky Division of Air, Waste, and Water, where inspectors operate to enforce environmental laws.

Later in my career, I worked at Wild Turkey Distillery managing environmental and safety programs. It was another opportunity to apply my environmental expertise in an industrial setting, ensuring compliance with laws and promoting sustainability. I enjoyed helping the company maintain a conscientious public image.

I worked for Kentucky American Water at one point, applying my environmental skills in the water industry. It was part of my varied career after leaving government and consulting roles. The job allowed me to focus on environmental compliance and water resource management.

London, Kentucky, is home to one of the thirteen field offices of the Kentucky Division of Air, Waste, and Water. These offices house inspectors who monitor environmental compliance across the state. While I didn't work directly in London, it was part of the network supporting enforcement efforts.
